narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 69.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the lowest decile for grades in your third year of school, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure—far below the higher deciles typical among those who passed the bar. Your first-year grades, also in the bottom decile, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential element, signaling early struggles that the model sees as persistent risks compared to the mid-to-upper deciles common for successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 37 was a key counterbalance, ranking as the second-most important factor and nudging the prediction toward success, aligning closely with the average of 37.27 for those who passed. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 was lower than the 3.24 average for bar passers and your middle family income bracket is more common among them than lower ones, these played lesser roles here. Your choice to attend full-time matches the overwhelming majority of successful students, but it couldn't outweigh the early academic indicators. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by lagging significantly in school deciles, where they cluster higher, despite solid LSAT performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where low deciles strongly correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school rankings, perhaps targeting summer sessions or tutoring to climb those deciles. Strengthening your undergrad-level skills through targeted prep could also help, even if your LSAT is already competitive. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these gaps before reapplying—reach out to admissions for advice on appeal options or waitlist paths, and keep pursuing your goals with that full-time commitment. You've got strengths to build on; this is just one data point in your journey.
